mnemonics.title: Arrow Operator (->)
mnemonics.content: ARROW REMEMBERS: A pointer with ARROW goes RIGHT to LEFT - First dereference (->) then access member. THINK: "Arrow points TO the member".

mnemonics.title: Pointer to Structure Declaration
mnemonics.content: STRUCT PTR: STRuct - PTRactically think: '*' comes BEFORE variable name, NOT after struct. struct Student *ptr NOT struct Student* ptr.

mnemonics.title: malloc sizeof
mnemonics.content: MSM: Malloc Sizeof Must: ALWAYS use sizeof(structName) with malloc for structures. Count bytes before allocating.

mnemonics.title: Pass by Reference
mnemonics.content: MODIFY: MOdify DIn Functions - Pass address (&) and use pointer to MODIFY original. If you want changes, pass POINTER not value.

mnemonics.title: Self-Referential Structure
mnemonics.content: SELF: Structure Existing Links Forward - Contains pointer to SAME type, enabling chains/lists. Next must be POINTER not variable.

acronyms.usage: Dynamically allocates specified bytes, returns void* pointer to beginning of block. Always cast to structure pointer type.
Allocates memory for array, initializes all bytes to zero. Takes number of elements and size of each element.
Releases dynamically allocated memory. Always set pointer to NULL after freeing to prevent dangling pointer.
Creates alias/shortcut for complex type names. With structures: typedef struct { } Name; allows simple declarations.
Null pointer value indicates no address. Use to mark end of linked list or failed allocation check.

visualTips.visualization: Think of '->' as an arrow pointing FROM the pointer TO the member. p->name = (*p).name = arrow from p to name field.
Draw structure as contiguous boxes. Each box is a member. Add padding visually (empty spaces) to show alignment. Pointer points to FIRST box.
Draw node as TWO connected boxes: DATA box and POINTER box. Pointer box has arrow pointing to next node. NULL shown as X or grounded arrow.
Draw a house (structure) with pointer as a signpost pointing to it. Access members by reading sign and entering house to find specific room (member).

analogies.analogy: Like a house address written in your notebook. The address (pointer) lets you find the house (structure). To know who lives inside, you use the address to visit and check rooms (members).

Like a key (pointer) that opens a building, then you walk to a specific room (member). The arrow '->' is the combined action of unlock-and-walk. Without arrow, you'd have to write down exactly: unlock building (dereference) THEN walk to room (dot).

Static allocation is like reserving a fixed hotel room. Dynamic allocation is like calling hotel anytime to book any available room (malloc). Must check if room available (NULL check). Check out when done (free).
Like train cars where each car has a connector (pointer) to the next car. Each car (node) knows about the next car but not the whole train.
Giving someone your house key (pointer) vs giving them a photocopy of your house (value). With key, they can change anything. With photocopy, changes stay on paper.
